title: Hubble Space Telescope
subtitle: A mirror with a flaw that changed astronomy — from deep fields to the accelerating universe.

hero.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: the Hubble Space Telescope in low Earth orbit, massive golden solar panels extended, cylindrical silver body reflecting sunlight, Earth's blue limb visible below with thin atmosphere layer, deep black space background with stars. Hard sci-fi style, technically accurate proportions, dramatic lighting. Add the following text labels on the image: "Hubble Space Telescope", "Low Earth Orbit", "Solar Arrays".
hero.alt: The Hubble Space Telescope in low Earth orbit — cylindrical body with gold solar panels against the blue curve of Earth
hero.caption: Hubble orbits at approximately 540 kilometers altitude. Over three decades it has made more than one million observations and contributed data to thousands of scientific papers.

paragraphs: Some telescopes collect data. Others change the way humanity thinks about its place in the cosmos. The Hubble Space Telescope belongs to the second category. From low Earth orbit, over more than three decades, it has shown us galaxies billions of light-years away, weighed the dark energy filling the universe, read the chemistry of atmospheres around alien planets, and produced tens of thousands of images — some of which have become cultural icons.

But Hubble is also a lesson in what science does with failure. Launched in April 1990, it was almost immediately useless: its primary mirror had a flaw that blurred every image into smeared halos. Instead of abandoning the project, engineers and astronauts designed an optical correction and within three years transformed a crippled satellite into the most powerful astronomical instrument the world had ever seen.
Hubble is not the largest telescope ever built, nor the most sensitive. But it opened the universe at the precise moment humanity was ready to look.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: Space Shuttle Discovery launching at dawn in April 1990, massive orange external tank and white solid rocket boosters, brilliant exhaust plume illuminating the launch pad, Hubble Space Telescope visible in the open payload bay before deployment. Hard sci-fi style, dark pre-dawn sky, technically accurate. Add the following text labels on the image: "Space Shuttle Discovery", "STS-31 Mission", "April 1990".
image.alt: Space Shuttle Discovery launching in April 1990 — STS-31 mission with the Hubble Space Telescope in the payload bay
image.caption: Mission STS-31 aboard Space Shuttle Discovery carried Hubble to orbit in April 1990. Five astronauts deployed the telescope after several hours of on-orbit checks — and almost immediately received the first, disturbingly blurred, images.

body.heading: The mirror flaw: a catastrophe that became a triumph
paragraphs: Hubble's primary mirror is 2.4 meters across and was ground to nanometer precision — or so it seemed. A calibration error during the polishing process left the mirror's edge ground too flat by an amount comparable to one fiftieth the thickness of a human hair: approximately 0.002 millimeters. In everyday life, nothing. In a billion-dollar space telescope, this produced a condition known as _spherical aberration_, which scattered incoming light and blurred every image.

NASA faced an unprecedented situation. The telescope was in orbit, the mirror could not be replaced, billions of dollars had been spent, and the world knew about the failure. Rather than canceling the program, engineers proposed an elegantly simple solution: not fixing the mirror, but adding corrective lenses to the optical path — exactly as eyeglasses correct a human eye. The result was the instrument known as Corrective Optics Space Telescope Axial Replacement, a set of small mirrors precisely shaped to undo the mirror's error. Alongside it, the first servicing mission in December 1993 also replaced the Wide Field and Planetary Camera with a second-generation version that had built-in correction ground directly into its own optics.

Five astronauts completed the repair in five spacewalks — and it worked. The first images after servicing were stunning: Hubble had transformed from an embarrassment into an instrument capable of seeing across billions of light-years. It remains one of the most dramatic course-corrections in the history of science.

diagram.title: Hubble optical path: primary mirror and corrective optics

</svg>
diagram.caption: Simplified optical layout: starlight reflects off the primary mirror to the secondary, then passes back through a hole in the primary's center toward the instruments. The Corrective Optics Space Telescope Axial Replacement block, installed during the first servicing mission in December 1993, compensated for the spherical aberration of the flawed primary mirror.

body.heading: Five servicing missions: a telescope repaired in orbit
paragraphs: Hubble was designed from the start to be serviced in space — an unusual concept for a scientific satellite. Astronauts could swap science instruments, gyroscopes, batteries, and solar array panels. That flexibility saved the mission and turned what could have been a 1990s relic into a platform that was upgraded for two full decades.

The first servicing mission in December 1993 installed the corrective optics and new camera. The second, in 1997, added new spectrographs. The third, in 1999, replaced all six gyroscopes after four had failed — the telescope had gone into safe mode for several months. The fourth, in 2002, installed the most powerful camera to date — the Advanced Camera for Surveys — along with a new spectrograph. The fifth and final mission, in 2009, was the most ambitious: astronauts replaced two instruments that had failed on orbit, installed new gyroscopes and batteries, and refreshed thermal insulation blankets.
After the final servicing mission, Hubble was operating at peak capability. The telescope was nineteen years old — and the best observations were still ahead.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: two astronauts in white spacesuits performing a spacewalk servicing the Hubble Space Telescope, robotic arm of Space Shuttle visible, silver cylindrical telescope body with open instrument bay, Earth curved limb visible below in the background. No faces visible — silhouettes and back views only. Hard sci-fi style, dramatic orbital lighting. Add the following text labels on the image: "Spacewalk Servicing", "Instrument Bay", "Robotic Arm".
image.alt: Astronauts spacewalking to service the Hubble Space Telescope — open instrument bay, Shuttle robotic arm, Earth's limb below
image.caption: Across five servicing missions, astronauts spent more than 166 hours in total outside their spacecraft, replacing instruments, gyroscopes, and components directly in orbit.

body.heading: What Hubble discovered: from deep fields to dark energy
paragraphs: The list of Hubble's discoveries is long enough that any brief summary will be incomplete. But several results were genuinely revolutionary.

body.heading: Deep fields: looking back to the beginning
paragraphs: In 1995, astronomers pointed the telescope at a tiny patch of sky — roughly two thousandths of the full moon's area — that appeared completely dark between the stars. Hubble stared at it for ten days. The resulting image, called the _Hubble Deep Field_, was filled with thousands of galaxies, each containing billions of stars. The most distant among them appeared as they existed less than one billion years after the Big Bang.

In 2004, astronomers repeated the experiment with an even longer exposure — the Hubble Ultra Deep Field — and captured more than ten thousand galaxies in a single frame. These images restructured the field of galaxy astronomy: instead of a few hundred known structures beyond the Milky Way, it became clear that the universe is packed with galaxies in every direction, with estimates climbing toward hundreds of billions, then a trillion or more.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: the Hubble Ultra Deep Field — thousands of galaxies of all shapes, sizes, and colors against a pure black deep space background. Spirals, ellipticals, irregular shaped ancient galaxies. Some appear red-shifted (deep red) indicating great distance. Hard sci-fi style, scientifically accurate appearance. Add the following text labels on the image: "Hubble Ultra Deep Field", "10,000+ galaxies", "13 billion light-years".
image.alt: The Hubble Ultra Deep Field — thousands of galaxies of all shapes and sizes in a tiny patch of apparently empty sky
image.caption: The Hubble Ultra Deep Field (2004): a patch of sky the size of a pinhead held at arm's length. Every dot is a galaxy, not a star. Some of these galaxies existed only a few hundred million years after the Big Bang.

body.heading: The Hubble constant: measuring the expansion rate of the universe
paragraphs: One of the core reasons Hubble was built was to measure the Hubble constant — the number that describes how fast the universe is expanding. Through the late twentieth century, astronomers disagreed about its value by a factor of two. The Key Project — a large observing campaign that ran through much of Hubble's first decade — measured distances to dozens of galaxies using Cepheid variable stars, a class of pulsating stars whose true brightness can be inferred from their pulsation period. The result, published in the late 1990s: the Hubble constant is approximately seventy kilometers per second per megaparsec, with an uncertainty of roughly ten percent instead of the previous hundred percent.

That number has deep consequences: it determines the age of the universe, the scale of its largest structures, and ultimately its fate. Today the precise value of the Hubble constant is one of the hottest topics in physics: measurements from different methods disagree by more than their stated uncertainties allow. This conflict — called the _Hubble tension_ — points either to a systematic error in one of the measurement chains, or to new physics beyond the standard cosmological model.

body.heading: Dark energy: the universe is accelerating
paragraphs: In 1998, two independent teams of astronomers published a result that shocked the field: the expansion of the universe is not slowing down under gravity — it is speeding up. The conclusion rested heavily on Hubble observations of distant Type Ia supernovae, used as standard candles for measuring cosmic distances. The supernovae were dimmer than expected — meaning they were farther away than the gravity-dominated model predicted.

The cause of the acceleration was named _dark energy_ — a hypothetical form of energy that permeates space and acts against gravity. Today dark energy is estimated to account for approximately seventy percent of the total energy content of the universe, and it remains one of the deepest open questions in physics. The discovery earned the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2011, though Hubble's role in producing the data often goes unacknowledged in accounts that focus on the scientists.

body.heading: Exoplanet atmospheres: the first look at another sky
paragraphs: In the early part of the twenty-first century, Hubble accomplished something previously thought impossible: it detected the chemical composition of the atmosphere of a planet orbiting another star. The planet known as HD 209458 b — a hot gas giant that passes in front of its host star — allowed astronomers to measure how starlight filtered through its atmosphere. The spectrum revealed sodium, water vapor, carbon dioxide, and other molecules.

This technique — transit spectroscopy — is now the foundation for studying the atmospheres of hundreds of exoplanets. Hubble opened the door to a direction where its successor, the James Webb Space Telescope, continues to advance.

image.prompt: Scientific illustration for a science encyclopedia: a hot Jupiter exoplanet transiting its host star, starlight filtering through the planet's atmosphere shown as a spectral rainbow edge-glow around the dark planetary disk, molecular absorption bands illustrated as dark lines in the dispersed starlight spectrum below. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background, technically accurate spectroscopy concept. Add the following text labels on the image: "Host Star", "Transiting Planet", "Atmosphere", "Absorption Spectrum".
image.alt: Exoplanet transit: starlight passing through the planet's atmosphere leaves molecular fingerprints in the spectrum
image.caption: Transit spectroscopy — the technique Hubble first used to read an exoplanet's atmospheric chemistry. Molecules in the atmosphere absorb specific wavelengths of starlight, leaving characteristic absorption lines — a chemical fingerprint — in the observed spectrum.

body.heading: Hubble versus the James Webb Space Telescope: partners, not rivals
paragraphs: After the launch of the James Webb Space Telescope in 2021, a natural question arose: is Hubble still needed? The scientific community's answer is unambiguous: yes.

Webb is far more powerful than Hubble in the infrared — it sees through dust clouds, captures the earliest galaxies in the universe, and characterizes exoplanet atmospheres in greater detail. But Hubble is unique in the ultraviolet and visible wavelengths — ranges where Webb is nearly blind. Beyond that, Hubble's archive of more than three decades gives astronomers the ability to measure change over time: stellar proper motion, nebula evolution, flares from active galactic nuclei. When both telescopes observe the same target simultaneously, the result often exceeds what either can achieve alone.

diagram.caption: Hubble and the James Webb Space Telescope are partners, not competitors. Webb reaches farther in the infrared; Hubble remains unique in the ultraviolet and visible spectrum. Joint observations produce a more complete picture than either telescope can provide alone.

body.heading: Today and tomorrow: gyroscopes, safe mode, and end of mission
paragraphs: Hubble is an aging spacecraft. After more than thirty-five years in orbit, the telescope faces growing technical challenges. The most critical involve its gyroscopes, which maintain precise pointing. Of the six gyroscopes installed during the final servicing mission in 2009, only one was reliably functional by the mid-2020s. As a result, NASA switched Hubble to one-gyro mode, which limits the telescope's maneuverability and reduces the fraction of the sky it can observe at any given time.

The expected end of mission falls somewhere between 2030 and 2035. Hubble carries no dedicated deorbit propulsion: at its altitude, atmospheric drag slowly reduces the orbit. Eventually the spacecraft will reenter the atmosphere in an uncontrolled manner. A private servicing mission for controlled deorbit or orbit raising has been discussed, but as of 2026 no formally approved plan exists.

Under any scenario, Hubble's scientific legacy will outlast the hardware by decades. The data archive — more than 169 terabytes of observations — remains openly accessible to astronomers worldwide. More than ten thousand peer-reviewed papers cite Hubble data, and new ones appear every year even from observations made in the 1990s.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: the Pillars of Creation — towering columns of interstellar gas and dust in the Eagle Nebula, illuminated by intense ultraviolet radiation from newborn stars, dark dense clouds at the top of each pillar forming into new stars, rich colors: deep blue gas, orange-brown dust, pink hydrogen emission regions. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background. Add the following text labels on the image: "Eagle Nebula", "Pillars of Creation", "Star Formation", "7,000 light-years".
image.alt: The Pillars of Creation in the Eagle Nebula — towering columns of gas and dust where new stars are forming
image.caption: The Pillars of Creation in the Eagle Nebula — one of Hubble's most celebrated images (1995, revisited in 2014 and 2022). The columns of interstellar gas and dust, several light-years tall, are active star-forming regions.

body.heading: Cultural weight: a telescope as a mirror of humanity
paragraphs: Few scientific instruments become cultural symbols. Hubble is one of them. Its images have appeared on magazine covers, in art galleries, and in school textbooks across the world. The Pillars of Creation, the Tarantula Nebula, the deep fields — these are not merely scientific data sets. They are images that change the way people understand their position in the universe.

There is a particular irony in all of this: the telescope that began as a technical failure and a subject of late-night comedy became a symbol of scientific persistence. The mirror flaw was corrected. The gyroscopes were replaced. Every obstacle was overcome or worked around — and in the end, an orbital observatory launched near the close of the Cold War continues to reveal the universe in the third decade of the twenty-first century.

glossary.definition: An optical defect in which rays reflected from different parts of a mirror or lens converge at different focal points, producing a blurred image. In Hubble, it arose from a polishing error that left the primary mirror's edge ground 0.002 millimeters too flat.

An extremely long exposure of a small, apparently empty region of sky, revealing faint distant objects — galaxies billions of light-years away. The Hubble Deep Field (1995) and the Hubble Ultra Deep Field (2004) each contain thousands of galaxies in a patch smaller than one hundredth the area of the full moon.

A crewed Space Shuttle mission to Hubble to repair equipment, replace instruments, and upgrade components in orbit. Five such missions took place between 1993 and 2009 — an approach unique in the history of robotic scientific satellites.

A number describing how fast the universe is expanding: the recession velocity per unit of distance, measured in kilometers per second per megaparsec. Its precise value is the subject of active scientific debate, known as the Hubble tension.

A hypothetical form of energy filling all of space and acting against gravity, driving the accelerating expansion of the universe. Discovered in 1998 through Hubble observations of distant supernovae, it is estimated to account for approximately 70 percent of the universe's total energy content.

A technique for studying an exoplanet's atmosphere: as the planet passes in front of its host star, some starlight filters through the atmosphere, leaving a molecular fingerprint in the spectrum. Hubble first applied this method in 2002 to the planet HD 209458 b.

A class of stellar explosions with a known intrinsic brightness, used as a "standard candle" to measure cosmic distances. Hubble observations of distant Type Ia supernovae in 1998 provided key evidence for the accelerating expansion of the universe.

A discrepancy between values of the Hubble constant measured by different methods: distance-ladder measurements (using Cepheids and supernovae) yield a higher value than those derived from the cosmic microwave background. The tension exceeds measurement uncertainties and may point to new physics.

A class of pulsating stars whose intrinsic luminosity is tied to their pulsation period. By comparing true and apparent brightness, astronomers can measure distances. Hubble used Cepheid variables extensively in the Key Project to pin down the Hubble constant.

quiz.question: Why were the first images from Hubble, taken after its launch in 1990, blurred?
options: The telescope drifted through a micrometeorite swarm that damaged the optics
The primary mirror had spherical aberration caused by a polishing error during manufacture
The cooling system failed, causing the detectors to overheat
The solar arrays deployed incorrectly, leaving the telescope without enough power
quiz.explanation: The primary mirror was ground with an error of approximately 0.002 millimeters — the edge was polished too flat. This produced spherical aberration: rays from different parts of the mirror focused at different points, blurring all images. The problem was corrected in December 1993 by installing corrective optics and a new camera with built-in correction.

quiz.question: What did the Hubble Deep Field and Hubble Ultra Deep Field images reveal?
options: That intergalactic space between galaxies is completely empty
That most early-universe galaxies were far larger than modern ones
That even a tiny apparently empty patch of sky contains thousands of galaxies
That the Milky Way is the largest galaxy in the observable universe
quiz.explanation: The Deep Field (1995) and Ultra Deep Field (2004) showed that every tiny patch of sky that appears blank is in fact filled with thousands of galaxies. This fundamentally changed our understanding of the universe's scale and galactic population — estimates grew from hundreds of billions of galaxies toward a trillion or more.

quiz.question: What did astronomers discover in 1998 using Hubble observations of distant supernovae?
options: That the expansion of the universe is slowing down and will eventually reverse
That the expansion of the universe is accelerating, implying the existence of dark energy
That the universe is not expanding at all but merely rotating
That Type Ia supernovae cannot be used as standard candles because their brightness varies unpredictably
quiz.explanation: Hubble observations showed that distant Type Ia supernovae were dimmer than expected — meaning they were farther away than a gravity-decelerated model predicted. This demonstrated accelerating expansion. The cause was named dark energy. The discovery earned the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2011.

quiz.question: Why does Hubble remain irreplaceable even after the James Webb Space Telescope was launched?
options: Hubble is far more powerful than Webb in the infrared range
Hubble can observe solar system objects while Webb cannot
Hubble is the only large orbital telescope that observes in the ultraviolet and visible wavelengths
Hubble is cheaper to operate because astronauts can service it
quiz.explanation: Webb was designed for the infrared and is nearly blind in the visible and ultraviolet spectrum. Hubble remains the only large space telescope capable of ultraviolet observations — a wavelength range inaccessible from Earth's surface due to atmospheric absorption. That unique capability is preserved even alongside the far more powerful Webb.

quiz.question: What technical limitation is currently (2026) reducing Hubble's observing capability?
options: The primary mirror was damaged by a micrometeorite impact
The spacecraft has exhausted its orbital maintenance fuel
Most gyroscopes have failed, so the telescope operates in one-gyro mode
The main detector has failed and cannot be replaced without a crewed mission
quiz.explanation: By the mid-2020s only one of the six gyroscopes installed in 2009 remained reliably functional. NASA switched Hubble to one-gyro mode, which narrows the accessible sky area and reduces maneuverability. Despite this limitation the telescope continues to carry out scientific observations.
